name: Basoga
description: The Basoga inhabit the Busoga region on the northern shores of Lake Victoria, east of the Nile. Unlike their Baganda neighbours, the Basoga historically had no single kingdom but rather a cluster of chiefdoms loosely federated under a Kyabazinga (paramount chief). They are celebrated as one of Uganda's most musical cultures — the amadinda and embaire xylophones of Busoga are considered a national cultural treasure.
cultural_highlights: Embaire xylophone ensemble — a 12-22 key xylophone played by up to 5 performers simultaneously; central to Busoga royal and civic music
Kyabazinga institution — the paramount chieftaincy of Busoga, a 20th-century federation of the traditional chiefdoms
Naming ceremonies (Okuzaalibwa) — elaborate rituals three days after birth to introduce the child to the community and ancestors
Imbalu circumcision — shared with the Bagisu, male circumcision is a cornerstone rite involving public performance and community witness

food_traditions.name: Matoke with groundnut stew
food_traditions.description: Steamed green bananas with peanut sauce — daily staple shared with Buganda

food_traditions.name: Obukonde (cowpeas)
food_traditions.description: Boiled cowpeas seasoned with onion and tomato, often served alongside fish from Lake Victoria

food_traditions.name: Kawunga (white maize flour)
food_traditions.description: Maize ugali eaten with dried fish, beans, or leafy greens

music_traditions: Embaire ensemble — the largest and most prestigious xylophone tradition in East Africa, requiring years of training
Entengezi — a smaller royal xylophone used in court music and gentler ceremonial contexts
Ndere flute music — the end-blown flute (ndere) is played by herders and in quiet ceremonial settings
